title: ALD Oxidant as A Tuning Knob for Memory Window Expansion in Ferroelectric FETs for Vertical NAND Applications
summary: Dielectric inserts are widely used to expand the memory window (MW) in ferroelectric FETs (FeFETs) for vertical NAND applications, with prior efforts focused primarily on material selection and stack positioning. Here, we demonstrate that the ALD oxidant used for the Al2O3 interlayer serves as a process-level tuning knob for MW engineering. H2O-grown Al2O3 yields a significantly larger MW (7-8 V) compared to O3 (4 V) for both gate-injection (12/3) and tunnel dielectric (8/3/8) configurations. While the tunnel dielectric (8/3/8) stack maintains robust retention up to 1e4s at 125C despite the larger MW, the gate-injection (12/3) configuration exhibits pronounced retention degradation for the H2O case. The enhanced MW is attributed to higher interlayer leakage associated with H2O-based ALD. These results establish oxidant choice as a key process parameter for co-optimizing MW and retention in ferroelectric NAND technologies.

AI.tldr: ALD oxidant (H2O vs O3) in Al2O3 interlayer controls memory window and retention in FeFETs for vertical NAND; H2O yields larger memory window (7-8 V) than O3 (4 V) but can degrade retention in gate‑injection stacks.
AI.motivation: Expand the memory window (MW) in ferroelectric FETs for vertical NAND applications using dielectric inserts; prior work focused on material choice and stack positioning, prompting exploration of process‑level tuning knobs.
AI.method: Atomic layer deposition (ALD) of Al2O3 interlayer was performed using either H2O or O3 as oxidant in two stack architectures: gate‑injection (12 nm/3 nm) and tunnel‑dielectric (8 nm/3 nm/8 nm). Memory window and retention were measured for each configuration.
AI.result: H2O‑grown Al2O3 produces a significantly larger memory window (7–8 V) compared with O3‑grown Al2O3 (≈4 V) for both the gate‑injection and tunnel‑dielectric stacks. The tunnel‑dielectric (8/3/8) configuration maintains robust retention up to 10⁴ s at 125 °C despite the larger window, whereas the gate‑injection (12/3) configuration exhibits pronounced retention degradation for the H2O case. The enhanced MW is attributed to higher interlayer leakage associated with H2O‑based ALD.
AI.conclusion: Choice of ALD oxidant is a critical process parameter for co‑optimizing memory window and retention in ferroelectric NAND technologies.

title: Endohedral Derivatives of the Recently Synthesized Two-Dimensional Fullerene Networks: Electronic and Optical Insights from First-Principles Calculations
summary: The quasi-hexagonal phase of the two-dimensional fullerene network (qHPC$_{60}$), recently synthesized, has emerged as a stable carbon-based material with distinct structural and electronic features. In this work, we employed density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate the electronic and optical properties of its endohedral derivatives. The encapsulation of nitrogen, cerium, and strontium atoms inside fullerene cages was systematically analyzed at different concentrations. Our results show that encapsulation preserves the semiconducting backbone of pristine qHPC$_{60}$ while introducing localized electronic states that alter the bandgap and enable new transition channels. Nitrogen encapsulation produces intragap states with potential relevance for discrete optical emission, whereas cerium and strontium generate intraband states near the conduction edge. These modifications induce a red shift of the absorption onset into the visible spectrum, accompanied by enhanced refractive and absorptive responses. The robustness of the electronic structure under reduced concentrations indicates that the fully encapsulated limit adequately represents the system. Overall, the findings highlight impurity-endowed qHPC$_{60}$ as a promising platform for optoelectronic and light-harvesting applications.

AI.tldr: This study investigates endohedral derivatives of the quasi-hexagonal 2D fullerene network (qHPC60) using DFT calculations, focusing on nitrogen, cerium, and strontium atom encapsulation at various concentrations.
AI.motivation: The qHPC60 phase is a newly synthesized stable carbon material with unique structural and electronic properties. The research aims to explore whether endohedral doping can modify its electronic and optical characteristics for optoelectronic applications.
AI.method: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to systematically analyze the electronic and optical properties of nitrogen, cerium, and strontium encapsulated inside fullerene cages at different concentrations.
AI.result: Encapsulation preserves the semiconducting backbone while introducing localized states: nitrogen creates intragap states for potential optical emission; cerium and strontium generate intraband states near the conduction edge. These modifications cause red shift into visible spectrum with enhanced refractive and absorptive responses. The electronic structure remains robust even at reduced concentrations.
AI.conclusion: Impurity-endowed qHPC60 shows promise for optoelectronic and light-harvesting applications, with the fully encapsulated limit adequately representing the system's properties.

title: Intertwined Swirling Polarization States in BaTiO$_3$ with Embedded BaZrO$_3$ Nanoregions
comment: Accepted for publication in Phys. Rev. Lett
summary: Ferroelectric materials embedded with dielectric inclusions offer a unique platform for exploring novel topological polar textures. Using first-principles-based atomistic simulations, we investigate the polarization behavior of a BaTiO$_3$ matrix containing segregated BaZrO$_3$ nanoregions. We demonstrate that the polar texture in three-dimensionally ordered arrays of dielectric inclusions is governed by their size and spacing, revealing three distinct regimes. At large separations, the nanocomposite exhibits bulk-like BaTiO3 phase transitions, while at smaller spacings, interconnected swirling polarization patterns give rise to vortex supercrystal states. We analyze the stabilization mechanisms of these states and show that each regime is characterized by distinct switching behavior. Furthermore, we find that nanocomposites with randomly distributed dielectric inclusions exhibit swirling polarization textures, giving rise to an amorphous network of entangled vortices. Our findings provide new insights into the physics of relaxor ferroelectrics, are consistent with recent experimental observations, and open up new possibilities for designing materials with emergent topological functionalities.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates polarization behaviors in BaTiO3 matrix with BaZrO3 dielectric inclusions using first-principles-based atomistic simulations, revealing three distinct regimes governed by inclusion size and spacing, including vortex supercrystal states at small separations.
AI.motivation: Ferroelectric materials with dielectric inclusions provide a platform for exploring novel topological polar textures, and understanding the relationship between inclusion geometry and polarization patterns is crucial for designing materials with emergent topological functionalities.
AI.method: First-principles-based atomistic simulations were used to investigate the polarization behavior of BaTiO3 matrix containing segregated BaZrO3 nanoregions, examining both 3D ordered arrays and randomly distributed inclusions.
AI.result: Three distinct regimes were identified: (1) large separations show bulk-like BaTiO3 phase transitions, (2) smaller spacings produce interconnected swirling polarization patterns forming vortex supercrystal states, and (3) randomly distributed inclusions create amorphous networks of entangled vortices. Each regime exhibits distinct switching behavior.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into relaxor ferroelectric physics, are consistent with experimental observations, and offer new possibilities for designing materials with emergent topological functionalities.

title: Persistent short-range charge correlations revealed by ultrafast melting of electronic order in YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$
summary: Charge density waves (CDW) are ubiquitous in the complex phase diagram of cuprate superconductors and exhibit both short- and long-range correlations. Using time-resolved resonant X-ray scattering, we investigate the photo-induced dynamics of CDW in YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.67}$. We discover an excitation threshold ($Φ$$_\mathrm{C}$ $\approx$ 65 $μ$J/cm$^2$) above which long-range CDW disappear, revealing a persistent CDW peak with short-range correlation length. Ultrafast photo-excitation promptly uncovers this residual short-range CDW correlations, appearing within $\approx$ 0.2 ps. Long-range CDW coherence recovers within $\approx$ 0.6 ps, while the peak intensity remains partially suppressed. We rationalize the dichotomic behavior in the fluence and temporal dependencies as the signature of two coexisting CDW peaks, arising from short- and long-range correlations, which we disentangle through their distinct response to photo-excitation. We provide evidence that the collapse of long-range correlations is driven by an electronic process, while short-range correlations are characterized by distinct timescales and stiffness against photo-excitation. This approach establishes ultrafast X-ray scattering as an effective tool for disentangling coexisting density waves and correlations in quantum materials.

AI.tldr: Using time-resolved resonant X-ray scattering, this study investigates photo-induced dynamics of charge density waves (CDW) in YBa₂Cu₃O₆.₆₇, discovering an excitation threshold (~65 μJ/cm²) that reveals coexisting short- and long-range CDW correlations with distinct responses to photo-excitation.
AI.motivation: Charge density waves are ubiquitous in cuprate superconductors and exhibit both short- and long-range correlations, but disentangling these coexisting phases remains challenging. Understanding their distinct dynamics could reveal fundamental insights into quantum materials' complex phase diagrams.
AI.method: Time-resolved resonant X-ray scattering was used to investigate photo-induced dynamics of CDW in YBa₂Cu₃O₆.₆₇. Ultrafast photo-excitation was applied to probe the temporal evolution of CDW correlations.
AI.result: An excitation threshold (Φ_C ≈ 65 μJ/cm²) was discovered above which long-range CDW disappears while short-range CDW persists. Short-range CDW appears within ~0.2 ps, long-range CDW recovers within ~0.6 ps with partially suppressed intensity. The dichotomic behavior reveals two coexisting CDW peaks from short- and long-range correlations with distinct responses: long-range collapse is driven by electronic process, while short-range shows distinct timescales and stiffness against photo-excitation.
AI.conclusion: This approach establishes ultrafast X-ray scattering as an effective tool for disentangling coexisting density waves and correlations in quantum materials, providing evidence for the electronic nature of long-range CDW collapse and the robustness of short-range correlations.

title: Bias in Universal Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials and its Effects on Fine-Tuning
summary: Universal machine learned interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) embody a growing area of interest due to their transferability across the periodic table, displaying an error of about 0.6 kcal/mol against the Matbench Discovery test set. However, we show that achieving more accurate predictions on out-of-domain tasks requires fine-tuning. Additionally, we investigate the existence and influence of model biases in molecular dynamics (MD) by examining two approaches for data generation: from multiple MD trajectories in parallel, which we call naive fine-tuning, and from a single MD trajectory with fine-tuning after set intervals, which we call periodic fine-tuning. Our results find that naive fine-tuning generates constrained datasets that fail to represent MD simulations, and thus downstream fine-tuned models fail during extrapolation. In contrast, periodic fine-tuning yields models which are more generalizable and accurate, producing low-error dynamics. These findings indicate the role of uMLIP bias in fine-tuning, and highlights the need for multiple fine-tuning steps. Lastly, we relate unphysical behavior to principal component space, and quantify extrapolations through Q-residual analysis, which are useful as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty for larger simulations.

AI.tldr: This study shows that periodic fine-tuning of uMLIPs from single MD trajectories yields more generalizable and accurate models than naive fine-tuning from multiple parallel trajectories. The approach of using Q-residual analysis helps quantify epistemic uncertainty in larger simulations.
AI.motivation: While universal machine learned interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) show transferability across the periodic table with ~0.6 kcal/mol error on the Matbench Discovery test set, achieving accurate predictions on out-of-domain tasks requires fine-tuning. The authors sought to understand the existence and influence of model biases in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations.
AI.method: The authors investigated two data generation approaches for fine-tuning uMLIPs: (1) naive fine-tuning using multiple MD trajectories in parallel, and (2) periodic fine-tuning using a single MD trajectory with fine-tuning at set intervals. They analyzed unphysical behavior through principal component space and quantified extrapolations using Q-residual analysis as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty.
AI.result: Naive fine-tuning from multiple parallel MD trajectories generates constrained datasets that fail to represent MD simulations, causing downstream fine-tuned models to fail during extrapolation. In contrast, periodic fine-tuning produces models that are more generalizable and accurate, generating low-error dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that periodic fine-tuning from a single MD trajectory produces more generalizable and accurate models compared to naive fine-tuning from multiple parallel trajectories. The former yields low-error dynamics while the latter produces constrained datasets that fail during extrapolation. Principal component space analysis and Q-residual analysis are effective for identifying unphysical behavior and quantifying epistemic uncertainty in larger simulations.

title: Pauli-limited upper critical field and anisotropic depairing effect of La2.82Sr0.18Ni2O7 superconducting thin film
summary: We investigate the upper critical field and superconducting anisotropy of epitaxial La2.82Sr0.18Ni2O7 thin films, which show a sharp superconducting transition at Tc=31.6 K. Near Tc, superconductivity exhibits thickness-limited two-dimensional characteristics. Upon cooling, the out-of-plane coherence length decreases below the sample thickness of 6 nm, corresponding to a 3-unit-cell film, indicating a crossover to intrinsic three-dimensional bulk superconductivity. High-field transport measurements reveal large upper critical fields with a small anisotropy ratio gama~1.34, comparable to bulk Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates. At low temperatures, the in-plane (ab) upper critical field Hc2(ab) is strongly suppressed by spin-paramagnetic pair breaking and approaches the Pauli limit (Hc2(Pauli)=58 T), while Hc2(c) remains largely unaffected. This anisotropic Pauli limitation accounts for the reduced upper critical field anisotropy and supports the conclusion that superconductivity in these films is fundamentally three-dimensional bulk like. Our results highlight the essential role of spin-paramagnetic effects in shaping the high-field superconducting phase diagram of Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates.

AI.tldr: Researchers studied epitaxial La2.82Sr0.18Ni2O7 thin films (Tc=31.6 K) and found a crossover from 2D to 3D superconductivity upon cooling. The small anisotropy ratio (γ~1.34) results from anisotropic Pauli limiting - spin-paramagnetic effects strongly suppress in-plane Hc2 toward 58 T while out-of-plane Hc2 is unaffected, demonstrating 3D bulk-like superconductivity with essential spin-paramagnetic effects in the high-field phase diagram.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to investigate the nature of superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates - specifically to determine whether the superconductivity is two-dimensional (surface/interface) or three-dimensional (bulk), and to understand the high-field behavior and anisotropy properties which are crucial for understanding the fundamental superconducting mechanism in these recently discovered nickel-based superconductors.
AI.method: The researchers used epitaxial thin film growth of La2.82Sr0.18Ni2O7 (6 nm thick, ~3 unit cells), combined with high-field transport measurements. They performed temperature-dependent measurements to track the coherence length evolution and determine the dimensionality crossover, as well as angular-dependent magnetoresistance measurements to extract the anisotropy ratio.
AI.result: The key findings include: (1) A sharp superconducting transition at Tc=31.6 K; (2) Thickness-limited 2D characteristics near Tc that crossover to intrinsic 3D bulk superconductivity at lower temperatures when the out-of-plane coherence length drops below the film thickness; (3) A small anisotropy ratio γ~1.34, comparable to bulk nickelates; (4) At low temperatures, in-plane Hc2(ab) approaches the Pauli limit (58 T) due to spin-paramagnetic pair breaking, while out-of-plane Hc2(c) remains largely unaffected.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that superconductivity in La2.82Sr0.18Ni2O7 thin films is fundamentally 3D bulk-like, not 2D as initially suspected near Tc. The reduced upper critical field anisotropy (γ~1.34) is explained by anisotropic Pauli limitation - spin-paramagnetic pair breaking strongly suppresses in-plane Hc2 while out-of-plane Hc2 remains unaffected. These findings highlight the essential role of spin-paramagnetic effects in determining the high-field superconducting phase diagram of Ruddlesden-Popper nickelates.

title: Symmetric localization of $ν_{\text{tot}}=4/3$ fractional topological insulator edges
summary: Motivated by the recent twisted MoTe$_2$ experiment [arXiv:2601.18508], we develop a disordered interacting edge theory of a fractional topological insulator at $ν_{\text{tot}}=4/3$, consisting of two time-reversal-conjugated $ν=2/3$ fractional quantum Hall states. For an $S_z$-conserving edge, we uncover three distinct phases with two possible conductance values per edge in the long-edge limit: $\frac{2}{3}\frac{e^2}{h}$ and $\frac{4}{3}\frac{e^2}{h}$. In the presence of $S_z$-changing perturbations (e.g., Rashba spin-orbit coupling), an interaction-induced insulating edge state can emerge without breaking time-reversal or charge-conservation symmetry, corresponding to the absence of a topologically protected edge state. We further provide an exact mapping to a noninteracting fermionic theory exhibiting Anderson localization. Our results showcase an explicit, experimentally relevant example that the edge-state two-terminal transport is insufficient to identify the $ν_{\text{tot}}=4/3$ fractional topological insulators.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a disordered interacting edge theory for a fractional topological insulator at ν_tot=4/3 (two time-reversal-conjugated ν=2/3 FQH states), finding three distinct phases with conductance values 2/3 e²/h and 4/3 e²/h. An interaction-induced insulating edge state can emerge with S_z-changing perturbations. The key conclusion is that edge-state two-terminal transport alone is insufficient to identify ν_tot=4/3 fractional topological insulators.
AI.motivation: Motivated by recent twisted MoTe2 experiment (arXiv:2601.18508), this work aims to understand the edge state transport properties of fractional topological insulators at ν_tot=4/3, which consists of two time-reversal-conjugated ν=2/3 fractional quantum Hall states.
AI.method: Developed a disordered interacting edge theory for a fractional topological insulator at ν_tot=4/3, using exact mapping to a noninteracting fermionic theory exhibiting Anderson localization. Analyzed S_z-conserving edges and edges with S_z-changing perturbations (e.g., Rashba spin-orbit coupling).
AI.result: Three distinct phases were uncovered for S_z-conserving edges with two possible conductance values per edge in the long-edge limit: 2/3 e²/h and 4/3 e²/h. In the presence of S_z-changing perturbations (Rashba spin-orbit coupling), an interaction-induced insulating edge state can emerge without breaking time-reversal or charge-conservation symmetry.
AI.conclusion: The edge-state two-terminal transport is insufficient to uniquely identify ν_tot=4/3 fractional topological insulators, as different phases can exhibit the same conductance values. This work provides an experimentally relevant example demonstrating the limitations of transport measurements in identifying topological phases.

title: Engineering photomagnetism in collinear van der Waals antiferromagnets
summary: Achieving efficient ultrafast optical control of antiferromagnetic spin dynamics is a central goal for next-generation high-speed THz spintronic and magnonic devices. Resonant optical pumping of crystal-field-split d-d orbital multiplets in magnetic TM ions directly modulates exchange and spin-orbit interactions, inducing large-amplitude coherent spin precession. However, such effects are limited to a handful of systems and there is no general strategy to enhance d-d photomagnetism in antiferromagnets. Here, we demonstrate the engineering of photomagnetism via TM-ion doping in collinear van der Waals antiferromagnets. In Mn$_{1-x}$Ni$_x$PS$_3$, small amounts of Ni$^{2+}$ activate a strong photomagnetic response while largely preserving the Néel ground state. Even 10% Ni boosts the response by more than an order of magnitude compared to pure MnPS$_3$, with resonant pumping of Ni$^{2+}$ d-d transitions driving large-amplitude coherent spin precession and providing helicity-dependent phase control. Tuning the pump energy across the full Mn$_{1-x}$Ni$_x$PS$_3$ composition range shows that Ni excitations remain effective across competing Néel and zig-zag antiferromagnetic states while supporting tunable-frequency coherent spin precession. These results establish TM-ion doping as a versatile strategy to harness orbital multiplet excitations for ultrafast, low-dissipation spin control in van der Waals antiferromagnets.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that TM-ion doping (e.g., Ni2+ in MnPS3) can significantly enhance photomagnetic response in van der Waals antiferromagnets by over an order of magnitude, enabling efficient ultrafast optical control of spin dynamics for THz spintronic devices.
AI.motivation: Achieving efficient ultrafast optical control of antiferromagnetic spin dynamics for next-generation high-speed THz spintronic and magnonic devices. The problem is that such effects are limited to a handful of systems and there is no general strategy to enhance d-d photomagnetism in antiferromagnets.
AI.method: Engineering of photomagnetism via TM-ion doping in collinear van der Waals antiferromagnets, specifically Mn1-xNixPS3 with small amounts of Ni2+ doping. Pump energy was tuned across the full composition range to study the effects.
AI.result: Small amounts of Ni2+ activate a strong photomagnetic response while largely preserving the Néel ground state. 10% Ni boosts the response by more than an order of magnitude compared to pure MnPS3. Resonant pumping of Ni2+ d-d transitions drives large-amplitude coherent spin precession and provides helicity-dependent phase control. Ni excitations remain effective across competing Néel and zig-zag antiferromagnetic states while supporting tunable-frequency coherent spin precession.
AI.conclusion: TM-ion doping is established as a versatile strategy to harness orbital multiplet excitations for ultrafast, low-dissipation spin control in van der Waals antiferromagnets.

title: Gap structure and phase diagram of twisted bilayer cuprates from a microscopic perspective
summary: Since the prediction of a time-reversal symmetry breaking (TRSB) $d+id^\prime$ state in twisted bilayer cuprate superconductors by Can et al. in 2021, several experiments have attempted to detect this state, yielding conflicting results. At present, it is not clear which differences in samples or experimental conditions might explain these discrepancies. In this work, we perform a tight-binding lattice model calculation with phenomenological interlayer tunneling, examining the order parameter as a function of twist angle, interlayer tunneling, doping, and temperature. We observe the TRSB state to be correlated to the position of the Van Hove singularity in the normal state which changes not only as a function of doping but also the tunneling strength. Two such phases are identified as nominally consistent with in-plane $d+id'$ and $d+is$ order, but with unexpected transformation properties under bilayer symmetry operations. We calculate the Josephson critical current, in particular examining the angle dependence for various tunneling strengths. Finally, we discuss the existing experiments in the context of our results.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the time-reversal symmetry breaking d+id' state in twisted bilayer cuprate superconductors using tight-binding calculations, finding that the TRSB state depends on Van Hove singularity position which is influenced by both doping and interlayer tunneling strength, potentially explaining conflicting experimental observations.
AI.motivation: Since the 2021 prediction of a TRSB d+id' state in twisted bilayer cuprate superconductors, experiments have yielded conflicting results regarding its detection, and the reasons for these discrepancies remain unclear.
AI.method: Tight-binding lattice model calculation with phenomenological interlayer tunneling, examining order parameters as functions of twist angle, interlayer tunneling, doping, and temperature; calculation of Josephson critical current with angle dependence.
AI.result: The TRSB state is correlated with the Van Hove singularity position, which changes with both doping and tunneling strength. Two phases consistent with in-plane d+id' and d+is order were identified, but with unexpected transformation properties under bilayer symmetry operations. The Josephson critical current shows angle dependence that varies with tunneling strength.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a framework to understand conflicting experimental results by showing how the TRSB state depends on both doping and tunneling strength, and offers predictions for Josephson critical current measurements that could help resolve experimental discrepancies.

title: Classical Kitaev model in a magnetic field
summary: Motivated by experiments on spin-orbit coupled magnets with Kitaev exchange in magnetic fields, we present an analysis of the classical Kitaev honeycomb model in the presence of a magnetic field. We show that there is a spin liquid regime that exists within a finite window of fields from zero up to a finite threshold before transitioning into the polarized paramagnet. We uncover constraints that spins need to satisfy in the ground state and show that they determine the exact limiting zero temperature behavior of the heat capacity and magnetic susceptibility within the spin liquid as a function of field. When the field is finite, both the two-point spin and the quadrupolar correlations are short-ranged, in contrast to the zero-field case. We rationalize an effective mass for the quadrupolar correlations in terms of a coarse-grained theory with fluctuating effective charge degrees of freedom. Finally, we show that weak site-dilution does not change the magnetization within the spin liquid -- a kind of "perfect" compensation of the site dilution.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the classical Kitaev honeycomb model in a magnetic field and discovers a spin liquid phase existing in a finite field window, with exact thermodynamic properties determined by spin constraints, short-ranged correlations, and a surprising "perfect" compensation effect where weak site-dilution does not affect magnetization.
AI.motivation: Experiments on spin-orbit coupled magnets with Kitaev exchange in magnetic fields motivated this analysis of the classical Kitaev honeycomb model in the presence of a magnetic field.
AI.method: Analysis of the classical Kitaev honeycomb model in a magnetic field, including constraints analysis on ground state spins, and development of a coarse-grained theory with fluctuating effective charge degrees of freedom.
AI.result: A spin liquid regime was found to exist within a finite window of fields from zero up to a finite threshold before transitioning into the polarized paramagnet. Constraints on spins in the ground state were uncovered and shown to determine the exact limiting zero temperature behavior of heat capacity and magnetic susceptibility within the spin liquid as a function of field. When field is finite, both two-point spin and quadrupolar correlations are short-ranged, in contrast to the zero-field case. An effective mass for quadrupolar correlations was rationalized. Weak site-dilution does not change magnetization within the spin liquid, representing a "perfect" compensation effect.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a stable spin liquid phase in the classical Kitaev honeycomb model exists in a finite field window, with short-ranged correlations and exact solvable thermodynamic properties. Additionally, weak site-dilution has no effect on magnetization in this phase, demonstrating a unique compensation effect.

title: Flexible Cutoff Learning: Optimizing Machine Learning Potentials After Training
summary: We introduce Flexible Cutoff Learning (FCL), a method for training machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) whose cutoff radii can be adjusted after training. Unlike conventional MLIPs that fix the cutoff radius during training, FCL models are trained by randomly sampling cutoff radii independently for each atom. The resulting model can then be deployed with different per-atom cutoff radii depending on the application, enabling application-specific optimization of the accuracy-cost tradeoff. Using a differentiable cost model, these per-atom cutoffs can be optimized for specific target systems after training. We demonstrate FCL with a modified MACE architecture trained on the MAD dataset. For a subset featuring molecular crystals, optimized per-atom cutoffs reduce computational cost by more than 60% while increasing force errors by less than 1%. These results show that FCL enables training of a single general-purpose MLIP that can be adapted to diverse applications through post-training cutoff optimization, eliminating the need for retraining.

AI.tldr: Flexible Cutoff Learning (FCL) enables training of MLIPs with adjustable cutoff radii after training, using randomly sampled cutoff radii during training and differentiable cost model optimization for per-atom cutoffs.
AI.motivation: Conventional MLIPs fix cutoff radii during training, requiring retraining for different accuracy-cost tradeoffs. This limits flexibility for diverse applications.
AI.method: Train models by randomly sampling cutoff radii per atom, then use differentiable cost model to optimize per-atom cutoffs post-training. Implemented with modified MACE architecture on MAD dataset.
AI.result: For molecular crystals, optimized per-atom cutoffs reduce computational cost by >60% while increasing force errors by <1%.
AI.conclusion: FCL enables a single general-purpose MLIP to be adapted to diverse applications through post-training cutoff optimization, eliminating need for retraining.

title: Supercurrents in Josephson junctions with chiral molecular potentials
summary: The influence of chiral molecular potentials on phase-coherent transport in superconducting Josephson junctions is investigated. Within a Bogoliubov-de Gennes tight-binding framework, an SNS junction functionalized by adsorbed chiral molecules is modeled, where electrostatic gradients generated by the molecules induce spin-orbit coupling in the normal region. The equilibrium charge current-phase relation is found to remain largely insensitive to molecular chirality in symmetric, zero-field configurations. In contrast, the spin supercurrent exhibits a pronounced chirality-dependent response, with opposite enantiomers producing distinct and anisotropic spin-polarized Josephson currents. The resulting handedness contrast can be enhanced through control parameters such as molecular orientation and the strength of the induced spin-orbit coupling. The temperature dependence of these currents further shows that the chirality-dependent signatures persist across a range of temperatures well below the superconducting critical temperature. These results establish Josephson interferometry as a phase-sensitive and accessible platform for detecting molecular chirality and highlight spin-polarized superconducting transport as a promising route toward integrating chiral molecular functionality into superconducting spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates chiral molecular potential effects on superconducting Josephson junctions using Bogoliubov-de Gennes theory. While charge current shows little chirality dependence, spin supercurrent exhibits distinct responses to molecular handedness, enabling chirality detection through Josephson interferometry. The effect persists at low temperatures and can be enhanced by adjusting molecular orientation and spin-orbit coupling strength.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate how chiral molecular potentials affect phase-coherent transport in superconducting Josephson junctions and to develop a method for detecting molecular chirality using superconducting spintronics.
AI.method: Bogoliubov-de Gennes tight-binding framework was used to model an SNS junction functionalized by adsorbed chiral molecules. Electrostatic gradients from molecules induce spin-orbit coupling in the normal region. The study examined charge and spin supercurrent responses to molecular chirality, enantiomer orientation, SO coupling strength, and temperature.
AI.result: The equilibrium charge current-phase relation remains largely insensitive to molecular chirality in symmetric, zero-field configurations. However, the spin supercurrent exhibits a pronounced chirality-dependent response, with opposite enantiomers producing distinct and anisotropic spin-polarized Josephson currents. This handedness contrast can be enhanced through control parameters such as molecular orientation and SO coupling strength. The chirality-dependent signatures persist across a range of temperatures well below Tc.
AI.conclusion: Josephson interferometry provides a phase-sensitive platform for detecting molecular chirality through spin-polarized transport. The chirality-dependent signatures in spin supercurrent persist across temperatures below Tc, offering a promising route for integrating chiral molecular functionality into superconducting spintronic devices.

title: Disorder-induced localisation in the Mott-Hubbard model
summary: For the Fermi-Hubbard model in the Mott insulator phase, we employ the hierarchy of correlations to study how doublon and holon quasi-particle excitations are affected by adding disorder to the system. We study two types of disorder: charge disorder, in the form of on-site potential randomness; and spin disorder, in the form of a fixed, randomly generated background spin arrangement. By analysing the quasi-particle eigen-spectra and quantifying the degree to which the corresponding eigen-states localise, we find both an energetic and spatial separation between localised and delocalised states in the charge disorder. In contrast, the spin disorder results in localised states throughout the quasi-particle bands. Finally, we repeat our calculations using strong-coupling perturbation theory, and compare the results obtained from both methods.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how disorder affects doublon and holon quasi-particle excitations in the Mott insulator phase of the Fermi-Hubbard model, comparing charge disorder (on-site potential randomness) and spin disorder (random spin arrangements), along with comparing hierarchical correlation methods to strong-coupling perturbation theory.
AI.motivation: Understanding the interplay between strong correlations and disorder is crucial for studying Mott insulators. While the clean Fermi-Hubbard model is well-understood, the effects of different types of disorder on quasi-particle excitations remain unclear, particularly regarding localization phenomena.
AI.method: The authors employ the hierarchy of correlations method to analyze quasi-particle eigen-spectra and quantify eigenstate localization. They also use strong-coupling perturbation theory for comparison. Two disorder types are investigated: charge disorder (random on-site potentials) and spin disorder (random background spin configurations).
AI.result: For charge disorder, both energetic and spatial separation between localized and delocalized states is observed. In contrast, spin disorder results in localized states throughout the quasi-particle bands. The strong-coupling perturbation theory results are compared with the hierarchy of correlations approach.
AI.conclusion: Different disorder types have distinct effects on quasi-particle excitations in Mott insulators: charge disorder allows for a mobility edge separating localized and delocalized states, while spin disorder causes complete localization. The comparison between methods validates the hierarchical approach against perturbation theory.

title: Structural and Optical Characteristics of beta-Ga2O3 Implanted with Rare Earth Ions
summary:
We investigated the structural evolution and optical properties of beta-Ga2O3 crystals implanted with different rare-earth (RE) ions using channeling Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry, Positron Annihilation, Photoluminescence, and Photoluminescence Excitation spectroscopies. The studies reveal that implantation-induced disorder, accompanying phase transitions, and post-annealing structural recovery are largely insensitive to the implanted RE species. The defect microstructure is also found to be similar for all implanted RE ions.
  Thermal annealing does not completely remove radiation-induced defects but instead drives their rearrangement into larger defect complexes. Unimplanted (virgin) beta-Ga2O3 exhibits strong UV-visible emission attributed to oxygen vacancies, whereas the introduction of RE ions produces additional emission lines originating from electronic transitions within RE3+ ions.
  The results indicate that RE3+ ions are excited through the host conduction band, followed by non-radiative relaxation to the 4f excited states and radiative decay to the respective ground states. Fluence-dependent studies of Yb3+ reveal the onset of concentration quenching, while RE-related emission remains efficient even in the presence of substantial lattice disorder.
  These findings provide new insight into defect evolution in ion-implanted beta-Ga2O3 and clarify the excitation mechanisms of RE3+ ions, offering guidance for optimizing the optical performance of beta-Ga2O3:RE materials.

AI.tldr: Study of beta-Ga2O3 crystals implanted with rare-earth ions reveals that implantation-induced defects and structural recovery are similar across different RE species. RE3+ ions are excited via the host conduction band, and RE-related emission remains efficient even with substantial lattice disorder. Findings provide insight for optimizing beta-Ga2O3:RE optical materials.
AI.motivation: Investigate structural evolution and optical properties of beta-Ga2O3 crystals implanted with different rare-earth (RE) ions to understand defect evolution and RE excitation mechanisms
AI.method: Channeling Rutherford Backscattering Spectrometry, Positron Annihilation, Photoluminescence, and Photoluminescence Excitation spectroscopies
AI.result: Implantation-induced disorder, phase transitions, and post-annealing recovery are largely insensitive to RE species. Defect microstructure is similar for all RE ions. Thermal annealing drives defect rearrangement into larger complexes rather than complete removal. Virgin beta-Ga2O3 shows strong UV-visible emission from oxygen vacancies. RE ions produce additional emission lines from 4f transitions. RE3+ excitation occurs through host conduction band. Yb3+ shows concentration quenching. RE emission remains efficient despite lattice disorder.
AI.conclusion: RE3+ ions are excited through the host conduction band followed by non-radiative relaxation to 4f excited states and radiative decay to ground states. Yb3+ shows concentration quenching at high fluence. RE-related emission remains efficient despite substantial lattice disorder. These findings provide guidance for optimizing optical performance of beta-Ga2O3:RE materials.

title: Ab-initio superfluid weight and superconducting penetration depth
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to Physical Review B as a regular article on the 2nd of March 2026
summary:
Machine learning and high-throughput screening approaches to superconductor discovery require physically meaningful descriptors that capture essential physics while remaining computationally tractable. The superfluid weight is an ideal descriptor as it is a prerequisite for superconductivity, determines the magnetic penetration depth and the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition temperature in two-dimensional materials, may limit the critical temperature in unconventional superconductors through phase coherence, and reveals quantum geometric contributions to supercurrent transport. We develop a computationally efficient framework for calculating the zero-temperature, mean-field superfluid weight for uniform pairing from density functional theory band structures and Bloch wavefunctions. We separately evaluate the conventional contribution from band curvature and the geometric contribution from quantum geometry. To validate the method, we calculate London penetration depths for a few conventional superconductors (Al, Pb, Nb, MgB$_2$, LuRu$_3$B$_2$ and YRu$_3$B$_2$) and find good agreement with experiment after accounting for nonlocal corrections, strong-coupling effects, and sample quality.
  The conventional contribution dominates by orders of magnitude in these wide-band materials, as expected. This framework provides a foundation for large-scale screening of superconducting candidates and exploring quantum geometric effects in unconventional superconductors.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a computationally efficient framework for calculating zero-temperature mean-field superfluid weight from density functional theory band structures and Bloch wavefunctions, enabling large-scale screening of superconducting candidates and exploration of quantum geometric effects.
AI.motivation: Machine learning and high-throughput screening for superconductor discovery require physically meaningful descriptors. The superfluid weight is ideal as it determines magnetic penetration depth, Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition temperature, may limit critical temperature in unconventional superconductors, and reveals quantum geometric contributions to supercurrent transport.
AI.method: The authors developed a computationally efficient framework for calculating zero-temperature, mean-field superfluid weight from DFT band structures and Bloch wavefunctions. They separately evaluated the conventional contribution from band curvature and the geometric contribution from quantum geometry.
AI.result: The method was validated by calculating London penetration depths for conventional superconductors (Al, Pb, Nb, MgB2, LuRu3B2 and YRu3B2), showing good agreement with experiment after accounting for nonlocal corrections, strong-coupling effects, and sample quality. The conventional contribution dominates by orders of magnitude in wide-band materials.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a foundation for large-scale screening of superconducting candidates and exploring quantum geometric effects in unconventional superconductors.

title: Long-range magnetic order with disordered spin orientations in a high-entropy antiferromagnet
comment: 10 pages, plus references, 1 table, 4 figures, and Supplementary information, accepted for publication in Nature Communications
summary: Disorder in magnetic systems typically suppresses long-range order, promoting short-range states such as spin glasses and magnetic clusters. This is particularly prominent in high-entropy materials, characterized by the random distributions of local magnetic entities and exchange interactions. However, in rare exceptions, long-range magnetic order can persist in high-entropy systems, while the microscopic characters and underlying mechanisms remain elusive, especially the magnetic behaviors of individual elements. Here, combining neutron diffraction and resonant soft x-ray scattering, we have conducted an element-specific investigation into the magnetic order of a high-entropy honeycomb-lattice van der Waals material (Mn1/4Fe1/4Co1/4Ni1/4)PS3. Despite significant atomic disorder, long-range zigzag antiferromagnetic order is observed below 72 K, with all four transition-metal elements participating in a unified phase transition. However, the spin orientations of various elements are distinct, attributed to the competition between single-ion anisotropies and exchange interactions. Our findings showcase a novel form of long-range magnetic order with disordered spin orientations, which is synergically stabilized by distinct magnetic elements in a high entropy magnet, offering a new paradigm for understanding complex magnetic systems.

AI.tldr: A high-entropy honeycomb-lattice material (Mn1/4Fe1/4Co1/4Ni1/4)PS3 exhibits long-range zigzag antiferromagnetic order below 72 K despite significant atomic disorder, with all four transition-metal elements participating but showing distinct spin orientations.
AI.motivation: To understand how long-range magnetic order can persist in high-entropy systems despite significant atomic disorder, and to investigate the magnetic behaviors of individual elements in such systems
AI.method: Combining neutron diffraction and resonant soft x-ray scattering for element-specific investigation
AI.result: Long-range zigzag antiferromagnetic order observed below 72 K with all four transition-metal elements participating in a unified phase transition, but with distinct spin orientations for various elements due to competition between single-ion anisotropies and exchange interactions
AI.conclusion: This study reveals a novel form of long-range magnetic order in high-entropy magnets where disordered spin orientations are synergically stabilized by distinct magnetic elements, offering a new paradigm for understanding complex magnetic systems.

title: A fully solution-processed organic microcavity laser in the strong light-matter coupling regime
summary: Solid-state semiconductor lasers underpin technologies from telecommunications and data storage to sensing, medical diagnostics, and emerging quantum communication. Polaritons-hybrid exciton-photon states have further extended this reach, enabling room-temperature quantum effects such as low-threshold lasing and single-photon nonlinearities. Organic semiconductors are ideal for polaritonics due to their large exciton binding energy, strong optical nonlinearities, and straightforward processing, making them attractive for both classical and quantum photonics. While solution-processed organic films have been widely explored, their optical cavities have almost always been fabricated using vacuum deposition, limiting the realization of truly scalable and low-cost devices. Here, we report the first organic laser microcavities fabricated entirely by solution processing, which operate in the strong coupling regimeThe resulting platform can be driven reliably to high excitation densities, where we observe a reversible, interaction-driven redistribution of the polariton condensate, revealing a distinct polariton lasing behaviour in organic microcavities. Together, the fabrication approach and the observed lasing dynamics establish a route toward scalable polaritonic and quantum photonic technologies and provide new opportunities for studying nonlinear polariton physics in organic systems.

AI.tldr: First demonstration of organic laser microcavities fabricated entirely by solution processing, enabling strong coupling regime operation and revealing distinct polariton lasing behavior with interaction-driven condensate redistribution.
AI.motivation: Organic semiconductors offer advantages for polaritonics (large exciton binding energy, strong nonlinearities, simple processing), but existing devices rely on vacuum deposition for optical cavities, limiting scalability and cost-effectiveness. This work addresses the need for fully solution-processed polaritonic devices.
AI.method: Fabricated organic laser microcavities entirely through solution processing (as opposed to traditional vacuum deposition methods). The devices were operated in the strong coupling regime and driven to high excitation densities to study their behavior.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated the first solution-processed organic laser microcavities operating in the strong coupling regime. At high excitation densities, the devices showed reversible, interaction-driven redistribution of the polariton condensate, revealing distinct polariton lasing behavior in organic microcavities.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a scalable, low-cost fabrication approach for organic polaritonic devices and enables new opportunities for studying nonlinear polariton physics. The findings pave the way toward scalable polaritonic and quantum photonic technologies.

title: Observation of Kondo hybridization wave in UTe2
summary: Condensed matter systems with strong electronic correlations often manifest a variety of intertwined ordered phases of charge, spin, orbital and other degrees of freedom. As a prototypical strongly correlated electronic system, the Kondo lattice provides fertile soil for many fascinating quantum states, including quantum criticality, unconventional superconductivity, hidden order and topological Kondo insulator/semimetal. The foundation of Kondo physics lies in the hybridization between localized moments and itinerant electrons. Generally, the evolution of Kondo hybridization is characterized as a broad crossover rather than a phase transition. Thus far, an ordered hybridization phase has not been observed. Here, we use scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) to identify a translational-symmetry-breaking order of Kondo hybridization wave(KHW) for the first time on the surface of the spin-triplet heavy-fermion superconductor UTe2. The unprecedented phase of KHW manifests as a periodically modulated Fano lattice, accompanied by a commensurate charge density wave (CDW) and a pronounced energy gap opening near the Fermi level. This KHW-imprinted CDW has an intriguing real-space texture of complementary occupation of the heavy f and conduction charges, thereby forming a Kondo superlattice. The KHW is coexistent with superconductivity in UTe2, which may provide valuable insight into its controversial spin-triplet pairing symmetry and the underlying mechanism. Our first experimental evidence for an ordered hybridization state potentially sheds new light on the strong correlation physics of Kondo lattice system.

AI.tldr: First experimental discovery of a translational-symmetry-breaking order of Kondo hybridization wave (KHW) in the heavy-fermion superconductor UTe2, identified using scanning tunneling microscopy. This KHW manifests as a periodically modulated Fano lattice accompanied by a commensurate charge density wave (CDW) and energy gap opening near the Fermi level, forming a Kondo superlattice with complementary occupation of heavy f and conduction charges.
AI.motivation: The foundation of Kondo physics lies in hybridization between localized moments and itinerant electrons, but this evolution has historically been characterized as a broad crossover rather than a phase transition. An ordered hybridization phase had never been observed before, despite being theoretically predicted in some contexts.
AI.method: Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) was used to investigate the surface of the spin-triplet heavy-fermion superconductor UTe2.
AI.result: Discovery of a KHW phase - a periodically modulated Fano lattice with commensurate CDW, accompanied by a pronounced energy gap near the Fermi level. The KHW-imprinted CDW shows real-space texture of complementary occupation of heavy f and conduction charges, forming a Kondo superlattice. The KHW is coexistent with superconductivity in UTe2.
AI.conclusion: This first experimental evidence for an ordered hybridization state provides new insights into strong correlation physics of Kondo lattice systems and may offer valuable insight into the controversial spin-triplet pairing symmetry and underlying mechanism of UTe2 superconductivity.

title: Ab initio quantum embedding description of magic angle twisted bilayer graphene at even-integer fillings
summary: Magic angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBG) hosts narrow moiré bands with meV-scale energy splittings, making its correlated phases sensitive to both material parameters and modeling choices in low-energy downfolding. We develop an ab initio quantum-embedding workflow that derives interacting flat-band Hamiltonians from Kohn-Sham density functional theory (KS-DFT) of a relaxed, unstrained structure. Our model combines constrained random phase approximation (cRPA) screening, controlled double-counting subtraction, and an automated gauge-fixing procedure based on the selected columns of the density matrix (SCDM) that is compatible with symmetry-resolved many-body calculations. Solving the resulting models using Hartree-Fock (HF) and coupled cluster singles and doubles (CCSD), we recover robust insulating Kramers intervalley coherent (KIVC) states at charge neutrality ($ν=0$) and at electron doping ($ν=+2$). The main new physical effect appears on the hole-doped side: at $ν=-2$ we observe a fragile semimetal with a weak $\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}$ Kekulé modulation and enhanced intervalley-scattering peaks in the Fourier-transformed local density of states. Although the underlying KS-DFT band structure is nearly particle-hole symmetric, the effective interacting Hamiltonian exhibits a pronounced particle-hole asymmetry at $ν=\pm 2$ that we trace to momentum-dependent single-particle renormalizations generated by subtraction terms constructed from reference densities consistent with the KS-DFT filling. Our work provides a first-principles route for connecting microscopic electronic structure, screened interactions, subtraction choices, and scanning tunneling microscopy signatures in MATBG.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an ab initio quantum-embedding workflow to derive interacting flat-band Hamiltonians for magic angle twisted bilayer graphene (MATBG) from Kohn-Sham DFT. The method combines cRPA screening, double-counting subtraction, and SCDM gauge-fixing. Using HF and CCSD calculations, they find robust KIVC insulating states at ν=0 and ν=+2, but a fragile semimetal with Kekulé modulation at ν=-2, revealing unexpected particle-hole asymmetry arising from momentum-dependent renormalizations.
AI.motivation: MATBG exhibits narrow moiré bands with meV-scale energy splittings, making correlated phases highly sensitive to material parameters and modeling choices in low-energy downfolding. There was a need for a first-principles approach to connect microscopic electronic structure with experimental signatures like STM measurements.
AI.method: The authors developed an ab initio quantum-embedding workflow combining: (1) Kohn-Sham density functional theory (KS-DFT) on relaxed, unstrained structures; (2) constrained random phase approximation (cRPA) for screening; (3) controlled double-counting subtraction; (4) automated SCDM gauge-fixing procedure compatible with symmetry-resolved many-body calculations; and (5) solving using Hartree-Fock (HF) and coupled cluster singles and doubles (CCSD).
AI.result: The study recovered robust insulating Kramers intervalvalley coherent (KIVC) states at charge neutrality (ν=0) and electron doping (ν=+2). At hole-doped side (ν=-2), they discovered a fragile semimetal with weak √3×√3 Kekulé modulation and enhanced intervalley-scattering peaks in Fourier-transformed local density of states. The effective Hamiltonian exhibits pronounced particle-hole asymmetry at ν=±2, traced to momentum-dependent single-particle renormalizations from subtraction terms consistent with KS-DFT filling.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a first-principles route for connecting microscopic electronic structure, screened interactions, subtraction choices, and STM signatures in MATBG, revealing unexpected particle-hole asymmetry arising from the subtraction procedure itself.

title: Frontiers of atom probe tomography physics, data processing, and analysis
comment: 43 pages, 7 multicomponent figures, summary of workshop
summary: Atom probe tomography (APT) fills a crucial need in the characterization workflow of materials by its ability to inform the 3D chemical microstructure at the nanoscale. As with any characterization techniques, APT has strengths and limitations that inform the interpretation of the data. Therefore, a challenge for the materials characterization community, and the APT community in particular, is the need to establish repeatable and reproducible workflows around the APT data acquisition, reconstruction, analysis, and sharing, in order to inform interpretation. Data interpretation also requires the continued development of our understanding of the physical processes responsible for field evaporation. We review recent developments in the experimental analysis of field evaporation and in the modeling of field evaporation leading to new understanding of common artifacts observed in reconstructed data. We then discuss current challenges with data analysis, translation of results, and data interpretation in the absence of community-agreed standards, and therefore, the crucial need for standardization at every stage of APT research, from data collection all the way to data reporting. This perspective is a summary of the invited presentations and discussions that took place during a workshop (August 4-5, 2024, Alexandria, Virginia, USA).

AI.tldr: This perspective paper reviews recent developments in Atom Probe Tomography (APT), discusses challenges in data analysis, interpretation, and the critical need for standardization at all stages of APT research from data collection to reporting. It summarizes a workshop held in August 2024.
AI.motivation: The materials characterization community faces challenges with repeatability and reproducibility in APT data acquisition, reconstruction, analysis, and sharing. Additionally, data interpretation requires continued development of understanding of field evaporation processes. There is a lack of community-agreed standards for APT.
AI.method: This paper is a review and perspective based on invited presentations and discussions from a workshop (August 4-5, 2024, Alexandria, Virginia, USA). It reviews recent developments in experimental analysis of field evaporation and modeling of field evaporation.
AI.result: The paper discusses new understanding of common artifacts observed in reconstructed data, current challenges with data analysis, translation of results, and data interpretation in the absence of community-agreed standards.
AI.conclusion: There is a crucial need for standardization at every stage of APT research, from data collection all the way to data reporting, to enable repeatable and reproducible workflows and improve data interpretation.

title: Pairing and charge distribution in Emery ladders preserving the ratio of Cu to O atoms
summary: We study the Emery model (three-band Hubbard model) for superconducting cuprates on three distinct ladder-like lattices that are supercell of the CuO$_2$ plane and thus preserve the ratio of copper to oxygen atoms. Using the density-matrix renormalization group method we confirm that these Emery ladders are charge-transfer insulators for the hole concentration corresponding to undoped cuprates but become Luther-Emery liquids with enhanced pairing correlations upon doping. The preservation of the Cu to O ratio allows us to study the distribution of charges between these atoms in the Luther-Emery phase. We show that these Emery ladders can describe the relations between charge distribution, pairing strength, and interactions that have been observed in the Emery model on two-dimensional clusters and in experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper uses DMRG to study the Emery model on ladder lattices preserving Cu:O ratio, finding they exhibit charge-transfer insulator behavior when undoped and transform to Luther-Emery liquids with pairing correlations upon doping, consistent with experimental observations on cuprates.
AI.motivation: To understand the physics of superconducting cuprates using the Emery model (three-band Hubbard model) in a way that preserves the Cu:O ratio while being computationally tractable.
AI.method: Density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) method applied to three distinct ladder-like lattices (supercells of the CuO2 plane) that preserve the copper to oxygen atom ratio.
AI.result: Emery ladders are charge-transfer insulators at undoped hole concentration but become Luther-Emery liquids with enhanced pairing correlations upon doping. The charge distribution between Cu and O atoms in the Luther-Emery phase shows relationships consistent with experiments.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms that Emery ladders can describe the relationship between charge distribution, pairing strength, and interactions observed in the Emery model on 2D clusters and in experiments, providing a computationally tractable approach to study cuprate superconductivity.

title: Electron-phonon physics at the exascale: A hybrid MPI-GPU-OpenMP framework for scalable Wannier interpolation
comment: 30 pages, 13 figures; accepted in npj Computational Materials
summary: We demonstrate a highly efficient GPU implementation of the Wannier interpolation of electron-phonon matrix elements in the EPW code. Building on a systematic analysis of the computational complexity of the algorithm for electron-phonon interpolation, we designed a GPU porting strategy that integrates naturally into the current EPW implementation, and is seamlessly portable to NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs. We demonstrate this development via extensive benchmarks on conventional semiconductors such as silicon and monolayer MoS$_2$, as well as a large-scale application to topological stanene nanoribbons of width as large as 20nm, which was intractable with previous implementations. Compared to the single MPI parallelization scheme of EPW v5.9, the resulting hybrid MPI-GPU-OpenMP scheme achieves up to 29-fold speedup on leadership-class supercomputers equipped with NVIDIA and Intel accelerators, namely Vista at the Texas Advanced Computing Center, Perlmutter at the National Energy Research Scientific Computing Center, and Aurora at the Argonne Leadership Computing Facility. This framework also achieves nearly ideal scalability up to thousands of GPU nodes on the Aurora supercomputer. With this development, EPW is ready to support electron-phonon physics calculations on exascale platforms.

AI.tldr: Developed a highly efficient GPU implementation of Wannier interpolation for electron-phonon matrix elements in EPW code, achieving up to 29-fold speedup and enabling previously intractable calculations of 20nm stanene nanoribbons on exascale platforms.
AI.motivation: The previous EPW v5.9 implementation with single MPI parallelization was insufficient for large-scale electron-phonon calculations, making certain systems (e.g., wide nanoribbons) computationally intractable.
AI.method: GPU porting strategy integrated into current EPW implementation with hybrid MPI-GPU-OpenMP scheme, designed based on systematic analysis of computational complexity for electron-phonon interpolation; portable to NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs.
AI.result: Up to 29-fold speedup on leadership-class supercomputers (Vista, Perlmutter, Aurora); nearly ideal scalability up to thousands of GPU nodes on Aurora; enables calculation of topological stanene nanoribbons up to 20nm width.
AI.conclusion: This development enables EPW to support electron-phonon physics calculations on exascale platforms, making previously intractable calculations (such as 20nm topological stanene nanoribbons) feasible.

title: Formulation of intrinsic nonlinear thermal conductivity for bosonic systems using quantum kinetic equation
summary: Nonlinear responses in transport phenomena have attracted significant attention because they can arise even when linear responses are forbidden by symmetry, with the quantum geometry of Bloch wave functions playing an essential role. While such effects have been extensively studied in electric transport, similar quantum-geometric mechanisms are also expected to govern nonlinear thermal transport. In particular, thermal responses are crucial in bosonic systems such as magnons and phonons, which are charge-neutral quasiparticles. However, a consistent theoretical description of nonlinear thermal transport remains challenging because of the difficulty in the treatment of energy magnetization in higher-order responses with Luttinger's gravitational potential method. Here, we formulate the intrinsic nonlinear thermal conductivity of bosonic systems using a quantum kinetic equation approach that avoids Luttinger's method and naturally incorporates contributions from energy magnetization. We identify three distinct contributions to the nonlinear thermal conductivity: two expressed in terms of quantum-geometric quantities, namely the quantum metric and the thermal Berry-connection polarizability (TBCP), and a third determined solely by the band dispersions. Applying our formalism to a specific quantum spin model within linear spin-wave theory, we show that the TBCP term dominates the nonlinear thermal Hall effect in the absence of threefold symmetry. Our results differ quantitatively from those obtained using semiclassical theory, thereby highlighting the importance of quantum corrections beyond the semiclassical picture. These findings establish a general framework for intrinsic nonlinear thermal responses in bosonic systems and reveal quantum-geometric mechanisms underlying thermal transport beyond linear response theory.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theory for intrinsic nonlinear thermal transport in bosonic systems using a quantum kinetic equation approach. Three contributions to nonlinear thermal conductivity are identified: quantum metric, thermal Berry-connection polarizability (TBCP), and band dispersion terms. The TBCP term dominates the nonlinear thermal Hall effect without threefold symmetry, with results differing from semiclassical predictions.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear responses in transport phenomena can arise even when linear responses are forbidden by symmetry, with quantum geometry of Bloch wave functions playing an essential role. While extensively studied in electric transport, similar quantum-geometric mechanisms are expected to govern nonlinear thermal transport. However, a consistent theoretical description remains challenging due to difficulty in treating energy magnetization in higher-order responses using Luttinger's gravitational potential method.
AI.method: The authors formulated the intrinsic nonlinear thermal conductivity using a quantum kinetic equation approach that avoids Luttinger's method and naturally incorporates contributions from energy magnetization. They applied their formalism to a quantum spin model within linear spin-wave theory.
AI.result: Three distinct contributions to nonlinear thermal conductivity were identified: (1) quantum metric term, (2) thermal Berry-connection polarizability (TBCP) term, and (3) a term determined solely by band dispersions. The TBCP term dominates the nonlinear thermal Hall effect in the absence of threefold symmetry. Results differ quantitatively from semiclassical theory.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish a general framework for intrinsic nonlinear thermal responses in bosonic systems and reveal quantum-geometric mechanisms underlying thermal transport beyond linear response theory. The results differ quantitatively from semiclassical theory, highlighting the importance of quantum corrections.

title: Polarization transfer force on ferroelectric domain walls
summary: We investigate the dynamics of ferroelectric textures driven by polarization currents. We show that, ferrons, the quanta of collective polarization excitations, provide an exotic driving mechanism for domain wall (DW) dynamics, compared with their magnonic counterparts. By mapping the linear polarization dynamics of a DW onto a Schrödinger-like problem with a Pöschl-Teller potential, we show that polarization waves are fully transmitted and therefore do not exert a net force on the DW in the linear regime. However, intrinsic nonlinearities give rise to a negative radiation pressure that pulls the DW toward the source. This mechanism allows efficient DW control by optical excitation and temperature gradients with application potential in ferroelectric memory and logic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates ferroelectric domain wall dynamics driven by polarization currents, introducing ferrons as quanta of collective polarization excitations. Through theoretical analysis using a Schrödinger-like model with Pöschl-Teller potential, the authors demonstrate that linear polarization waves transmit fully without net force, but nonlinearities create negative radiation pressure that pulls domain walls toward the source, enabling new control mechanisms for ferroelectric memory and logic devices.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand how ferrons (quanta of collective polarization excitations) provide an exotic driving mechanism for domain wall dynamics, compared with magnonic counterparts in magnetic systems, with the goal of enabling efficient domain wall control.
AI.method: The researchers map the linear polarization dynamics of a domain wall onto a Schrödinger-like problem with a Pöschl-Teller potential to analyze polarization wave transmission. They also analyze intrinsic nonlinearities to understand their effects on domain wall motion.
AI.result: In the linear regime, polarization waves are fully transmitted and do not exert a net force on the domain wall. However, intrinsic nonlinearities give rise to a negative radiation pressure that pulls the domain wall toward the source.
AI.conclusion: This negative radiation pressure mechanism enables efficient domain wall control through optical excitation and temperature gradients, with significant application potential in ferroelectric memory and logic devices.

title: Theory of Many-Body Multipole Operators in Single-Centered Electron Systems: Two-Body Toroidal Monopoles in Spinless Orbitals
comment: 5 pages in the main text, plus 100 pages of supplemental material
summary: One-body multipole operators are defined as irreducible representations of rotational symmetry together with spatial-inversion and time-reversal symmetries, providing a systematic framework for classifying electronic internal degrees of freedom and for describing a wide variety of composite order parameters. While this formalism has been successfully established for the one-body operator space, a systematic classification of the many-body operator space, especially in interacting systems, remains an open challenge. In this paper, we extend the multipole formalism in the one-body operator space to the many-body operator space. By formulating fermionic creation and annihilation operators as spherical tensors and employing Clebsch-Gordan coupling combined with the exterior (Grassmann) algebra, we construct an irreducible decomposition of many-body operators that fully incorporates fermionic antisymmetrization. As a concrete application, we classify monopoles appearing in spinless many-body operators. In particular, we show that the electric toroidal monopole, a pseudoscalar breaking spatial-inversion symmetry, and the magnetic toroidal monopole, a time reversal-odd scalar, become active in spinless interacting many-body systems, although they are absent in the spinless one-body hybrid orbital space.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the multipole operator formalism from one-body to many-body systems by using spherical tensors, Clebsch-Gordan coupling, and Grassmann algebra to construct an irreducible decomposition of many-body operators that incorporates fermionic antisymmetrization. As an application, the authors show that electric and magnetic toroidal monopoles become active in spinless interacting many-body systems, although they are absent in spinless one-body systems.
AI.motivation: While one-body multipole operators are well-established for classifying electronic degrees of freedom and composite order parameters, a systematic classification of the many-body operator space, especially in interacting systems, remains an open challenge. This work aims to fill this gap.
AI.method: The authors formulate fermionic creation and annihilation operators as spherical tensors and employ Clebsch-Gordan coupling combined with exterior (Grassmann) algebra to construct an irreducible decomposition of many-body operators that fully incorporates fermionic antisymmetrization.
AI.result: The authors classify monopoles in spinless many-body operators and find that both the electric toroidal monopole (a pseudoscalar breaking spatial-inversion symmetry) and the magnetic toroidal monopole (a time reversal-odd scalar) become active in spinless interacting many-body systems.
AI.conclusion: These toroidal monopoles are absent in the spinless one-body hybrid orbital space, indicating that interacting many-body effects are essential for activating these order parameters. This demonstrates the power of the extended multipole formalism for discovering new physics in interacting systems.

title: Beyond geometrical screening in predicting two-dimensional materials
summary: This perspective overviews the family of two-dimensional (2D) materials, which have attracted significant attention due to their properties and potential applications, and discusses how novel 2D materials including van der Waals (vdW) and non-vdW 2D materials have been predicted so far. A few thousand 2D materials have been predicted to be exfoliable or dynamically/thermodynamically stable, whereas a few hundred 2D materials have been synthesized so far, highlighting a gap between the theoretical prediction and experiments. This perspective introduces the recent developments in predicting the synthesis of non-vdW 2D materials.

AI.tldr: A perspective paper reviewing the field of 2D materials, noting that thousands have been theoretically predicted but only hundreds synthesized, and discussing recent progress in predicting synthesis of non-vdW 2D materials.
AI.motivation: This perspective addresses the significant gap between theoretical predictions and experimental synthesis of 2D materials. While thousands of 2D materials have been predicted to be exfoliable or stable, only a few hundred have been successfully synthesized, highlighting the need for better prediction methods, especially for non-van der Waals (non-vdW) 2D materials.
AI.method: This is a perspective/review paper that overviews the field of 2D materials, discussing theoretical predictions and experimental synthesis progress. It introduces recent developments in predicting synthesis pathways for non-vdW 2D materials.
AI.result: Thousands of 2D materials have been predicted to be exfoliable or dynamically/thermodynamically stable, but only a few hundred have been synthesized experimentally, demonstrating a large gap between theory and experiment.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that predicting the synthesis of non-vdW 2D materials is an important direction for closing the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental realization of 2D materials.

title: Non-Collinear and Non-Coplanar Magnetic Orders in 1/1 Periodic Approximant to the Icosahedral Quasicrystal
comment: 15 pages, 19 figures, Selected as JPSJ Editor's Choice, Selected as JPS Hot Topics
summary: Ground-state properties of rare-earth based 1/1 periodic approximants of icosahedral quasicrystal are clarified theoretically on the basis of an effective model for magnetism taking into account uniaxial anisotropy arising from crystalline electric field. By performing numerically-exact calculation on the 1/1 approximant crystal with a lattice constant $a$=14.725 Å, we have determined the ground-state phase diagram for ferromagnetic interactions. The result shows that eight kinds of noncollinear and noncoplanar magnetic structures are stabilized, whose magnetic space groups are identified as $I_{\rm P}m'\bar{3}'$, $C2'/m'$ and $R\bar{3}$. We have clarified the degeneracy of each ground state, which is expected to be reflected in the numbers of the domains. By analyzing each state, the magnetic as well as topological properties are revealed. Our results are shown to explain the measured magnetic structures in the 1/1 approximants and the effective model is discussed to be useful for understanding the magnetic structures and their topological properties in broad range of rare-earth based 1/1 approximants.

AI.tldr: This study theoretically clarifies ground-state magnetic properties of rare-earth based 1/1 periodic approximants of icosahedral quasicrystals using an effective model with uniaxial anisotropy from crystalline electric fields. Through numerically-exact calculations on a crystal with lattice constant a=14.725 Å, eight noncollinear and noncoplanar magnetic structures were identified with magnetic space groups I_Pm'3', C2'/m' and R-3.
AI.motivation: Understanding the ground-state magnetic properties of rare-earth based 1/1 periodic approximants of icosahedral quasicrystals, which have complex crystal structures and exhibit interesting magnetic behaviors due to crystalline electric field effects.
AI.method: Numerically-exact calculations on the 1/1 approximant crystal with lattice constant a=14.725 Å, using an effective model for magnetism that incorporates uniaxial anisotropy arising from crystalline electric fields. The study determined the ground-state phase diagram for ferromagnetic interactions.
AI.result: Eight kinds of noncollinear and noncoplanar magnetic structures were stabilized, with magnetic space groups identified as I_Pm'3', C2'/m' and R-3. The degeneracy of each ground state was clarified, revealing magnetic and topological properties of each state.
AI.conclusion: The results successfully explain the measured magnetic structures in the 1/1 approximants. The effective model is demonstrated to be useful for understanding magnetic structures and their topological properties in a broad range of rare-earth based 1/1 approximants.

title: Microstructural Characterization of Nb3Sn Thin Films Using FIB Tomography
summary: The accelerating gradient of Nb3Sn superconducting radiofrequency (SRF) cavities is currently limited, and the underlying cause remains an open question in the field.
